seedingNote: Seed each decision with patternType: ARCHITECTURE_DECISION. Run seeding script when Elasticsearch is available.

decisions.context: Analytics task types need Redis-style atomic counters and idempotency locks.
decisions.proposed: IRedisService — name the interface after the provider
decisions.challenge: IRedisService leaks provider identity into the interface name. Same mistake as 'php-wordpress' in stackCoupling keys. Callers would be coupled to a specific technology by name, even though Memcached and Valkey implement identical semantics.
decisions.resolution: IScopedMemoryService — named for capability (scoped, ephemeral, fast key-value with TTL) not provider. Provider resolved via FREEDOM config.
decisions.principleApplied: Fabric interfaces named for capability, never for provider. The same principle that eliminated 'php-wordpress' keys.
decisions.teachingPoint: Before naming a fabric interface: can the name survive provider replacement? If renaming the provider would require renaming the interface, the interface name is wrong.

decisions.context: System intake pipeline needs to read codebases. GitHub MCP was the initial proposal.
decisions.proposed: GitHub MCP — connect via Claude settings, use community skill
decisions.challenge: GitHub MCP hardcodes one provider and bypasses the fabric. The AF pipeline cannot know or care whether the user's codebase is on GitHub, GitLab, Azure DevOps, or uploaded as a ZIP. GitHub MCP is appropriate for web analysis sessions where a human and Claude collaborate — not for topology node execution.
decisions.resolution: ICodeRepositoryService with providers: GitHub, GitLab, AzureDevOps, Gitea, LocalGit, ZipArchive. ZipArchiveProvider is the minimum — unblocks intake of uploaded codebases without remote credentials.
decisions.principleApplied: Solution scope gate: ADAPTATION (GitHub MCP) vs EXTENSION (fabric interface). When the adaptation hardcodes a specific provider the user might not use, EXTENSION is the correct choice.
decisions.teachingPoint: GitHub MCP is the right tool for Claude web sessions. ICodeRepositoryService is the right tool for AF pipeline execution. They serve different contexts.

decisions.capability: Execute API runtime context (projectId + runtimeHints vs stack label)
decisions.context: The execute API needed a way to specify the target runtime for generation.
decisions.challenge: Stack labels pre-author knowledge XIIGen doesn't have. 'php-wordpress' implies a known stack profile. The reframe established that runtime context comes from PROJECT_UNDERSTANDING (what the system IS) or from explicit provider selections — not from a label the caller guesses at. The label approach reintroduces the stack-profile model through the API.
decisions.resolution: projectId (links to PROJECT_UNDERSTANDING for known codebases) + runtimeHints (explicit provider overrides, values are provider names not stack labels). No stack-label string accepted.
decisions.principleApplied: Derive from system state, never from pre-authored labels. runtimeContext.fabricProviders captures what actually ran, which is more useful training signal than a guessed stack label.
decisions.teachingPoint: Any time a string parameter could be 'php-wordpress' or 'node-nestjs', it is the wrong abstraction. Replace with either a projectId (for known systems) or explicit provider names.

decisions.proposed: INCOMPATIBLE for WordPress — no native scheduler equivalent to Bull
decisions.challenge: 24h TTL is a concept: schedule a callback that fires after N milliseconds. Bull implements this via delayed jobs. Action Scheduler implements this via as_schedule_single_action(). Both implement the same concept. The incompatibility was at the mechanism level, not the design level.
decisions.resolution: IMPL_VARIES_WITH_PROVIDER + ISchedulerService. Mechanism-first stackCoupling with no stack keys. Provider resolved at runtime from FREEDOM config scheduler_provider.
decisions.principleApplied: Before declaring INCOMPATIBLE: is this mechanism-level or design-level? Mechanism-level → introduce fabric interface → IMPL_VARIES_WITH_PROVIDER. Design-level → document why concept cannot be expressed in target runtime.
decisions.teachingPoint: INCOMPATIBLE should be rare after mechanism-first rethinking. Most apparent incompatibilities are mechanism-level mismatches, not design-level impossibilities.

decisions.context: All existing contracts used 'php-wordpress', 'node-nestjs' as keys in stackCoupling.
decisions.challenge: Stack-keyed entries assume XIIGen knows in advance what system it's targeting. The system intake reframe established that runtime context is derived from PROJECT_UNDERSTANDING or FREEDOM config — not pre-authored. Stack keys are the old stack-profile model leaking into contract structure.
decisions.resolution: Mechanism-first stackCoupling: { mechanism, tier, fabricInterface, freedomConfigKey, concept, neutralConcepts, degradedNote }. No stack names anywhere in the contract.
decisions.principleApplied: Contracts are stack-neutral by construction. Runtime context resolves at execution time, not at plan time.
decisions.teachingPoint: If a string in a contract could be a stack name, it is wrong. Replace with a concept, a fabric interface, or a FREEDOM config key.

decisions.capability: System intake pipeline vs per-stack content authoring
decisions.context: The original Tier 2 was 'maintain WordPress stack profile' — 550 lines per stack.
decisions.proposed: Per-stack content packages: XIIGen's team authors WordPress genesis prompts, .NET prompts, etc.
decisions.challenge: The WordPress plugin is one instance of the general problem. Users bring existing systems — Laravel, .NET, Go, Python. XIIGen cannot pre-author profiles for every combination. Each project within a stack has different conventions. The library of stack profiles is unbounded manual work.
decisions.resolution: System intake pipeline: 6 genesis prompts written once, run against any codebase. ARCHITECTURE_SCAN, CONVENTION_EXTRACT, CAPABILITY_INVENTORY, INTEGRATION_ANALYSIS, IRON_RULE_DERIVATION, DERIVED_CONTEXT_ASSEMBLY. Produces project-specific PROJECT_UNDERSTANDING.
decisions.principleApplied: Solution scope gate: derive from system state (user's actual codebase) rather than pre-author stack profiles. The 6 genesis prompts are EXTENSION (add to existing infrastructure). Per-stack profiles would be NEW INFRA that never scales.
decisions.teachingPoint: When asked to support a new technology: does the user's codebase contain the answer? If yes, build intake capability, not a content library.

decisions.context: 17 named checks used NestJS-specific regex that failed on other providers.
decisions.proposed: Immediate AI concept checks replacing all regex
decisions.challenge: AI concept checks require sub-calls, which benefit from loop.handler for iterative patterns. loop.handler is Phase Two (P-3). Implementing concept checks before loop.handler exists is premature.
decisions.resolution: Two stages: Stage 1 (Phase Zero, now) — provider-keyed regex variants. Stage 2 (after P-3) — AI concept checks. Stage 1 keys on runtimeContext.fabricProviders not on stack labels.
decisions.principleApplied: Prerequisite order matters. Implement what is useful now without creating dead-end dependencies on infrastructure that doesn't exist yet.
decisions.teachingPoint: Stage provider-keyed checks are better than per-stack regex (which multiplies by language count) and achievable before loop.handler. They are not the final design but are a valid intermediate state.

decisions.capability: DPO triple runtimeContext — fabricProviders vs targetStack label
decisions.context: DPO triples needed runtime context to enable per-provider fine-tuning.
decisions.challenge: A stack label is a pre-authored guess about the runtime environment. 'php-wordpress' assumes the code was generated for a WordPress project, but it was actually generated against specific providers (action-scheduler, wpdb). The providers are facts; the stack label is an interpretation.
decisions.resolution: runtimeContext.fabricProviders: { ISchedulerService: 'action-scheduler', IScopedMemoryService: 'redis' }. This captures exactly what ran, enabling per-provider training data pools.
decisions.principleApplied: Training data must capture facts about what actually ran, not guesses about what the stack is. Provider names are facts. Stack labels are interpretations.
decisions.teachingPoint: DPO triples tagged with provider facts ('action-scheduler') enable targeted fine-tuning per provider. Labels ('php-wordpress') create noisy mixed training data.
